as I discussed with Rahul yesterday, if you end results depends on the defined mock behavior you don't have to check the method is called. 

So simply move 'mockNetworkDataRepository.findBySearchCriteria(searchCriteria) >> responseFromRepository' (without 1 *) to the given: block (possibly as a separate and: statement)

Because of the high volume of data that can be expected, we'd better use Slice instead of Page.

Page includes total number of records matching the criteria, but it comes with an additional overhead count request that we might not be able to afford. Slice does not have this total number of records. It should not be an issue as this information is not included in the API request response anyway I think.

See also section 4 at https://www.baeldung.com/spring-data-jpa-pagination-sorting
What could be the impact of the 'in' clause on performance ? Could it prevents from using the index ? If this is the case, should we have an '=' condition ?

Either one of these two must be provided. That is why this condition is checking if both are missing.

blank line prefix, the sample on our Groovy convention page do use blank before each block (I dont mind either way, probably do prefer it with large blocks. But would like to see consistency in a single test...
(if you decide to use blank line don't use them before 'and:' labels as those are really part of the same block

it took me a while to see that the tets call this 3 times without any input parameter change which is unusual but make sense as you are testing pagination...

Maybe add something to the label like 'fetch data (repeated 3 times)
expectNextPageable
expectPreviousPageable
expectedPageNumber
use double || (they are all output variables)

use double || to separate input parameters from expectations
e.g.
scenario | input1 | input2 | input3 || expect1 | expect2

dont create columns for data that is the same in each or

in this case (when you have only one column you can use <<
e.g.

simplePayloadFileter << [ '...', '...' ]
I'm not sure if 'test' is really needed here. I would think it is redundant as we are obviously in a test class but it is a matter of personal choice...

I wouldn't use the word 'RELOAD' as that describes the action in the tets when you use this file.
I would like to see a more descriptive name though
maybe NETWORK_DATA_CHANGES
typo: should be NetworkDataQueryRepository

The intention of this variable is for end-user to decide what point in time they want to consider when fetching the data. I think from usage perspective it is a good name. But the same variable name 'pointInTime' becomes a little confusing in the implementation and I think createdBefore is a better name.

I find it is okay to keep the different names as they are being seen by different set of users.  But if we want to keep it same then I would prefer pointInTime over createdBefore in the implementation too.

Are we not loading in data in cps-temporal the same way we do in cps-core in regard to using liquibase changelog yml files. 

I presume the advantage to this is we can track every change that gets made to data and records within the DB. But if we decided to take a different approach with temporal that's fine too.

This data is only used for testing and cps is also following similar approach for test data.

https://gerrit.onap.org/r/gitweb?p=cps.git;a=tree;f=cps-ri/src/test/resources/data;h=f3c3db004028550a890625e5513a2d3c75b70955;hb=refs/heads/master

I'm now worry about the flexibility given here, still from a performance perspective.

We are good with order by observed_timestamps desc, but if order by is not corresponding to a defined index and no index can be used and response time could be an issue.

I think that only the options that are going to use the existing indexes should be supported.

The mandatory max row limit coming from the slide is also ensuring that the number of records retrieved is limited.
The order by clause is also playing a key role for performance. See other comment in the same file.

Summary does not comply with guidelines.

https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/Commit+Messages
I changed it but let me know if I am missing something.

We don't have index only on observed_timestamp field, so I am not sure if order by will be able use it. 
I have to read more about the impact of index in sorting. It sounds logical and should help if column is indexed but not sure how it will behave when the field is a part of a composite index.
OK it is your interface, was just point out the inconsistency :-)
